title: The Spiritual Bypassing of 'Just Let It Go'
dek: *You've been told that holding onto anger is like drinking poison and expecting the other person to die. But what if the real poison is the pressure to release what you haven't yet been allowed to feel? This is the quiet violence of spiritual bypassing, and it's keeping you stuck in the very patterns you're trying to escape.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Spiritual bypassing uses "let it go" language to avoid processing legitimate anger, which prevents healing and keeps trauma locked in the body. Anger carries information about boundaries, values, and unmet needs that must be heard before it can be released. True emotional maturity includes anger as sacred data, not something to transcend prematurely.

You've been told that anger is a low vibration you need to transcend. Someone hurt you. Someone violated your trust, dismissed your pain, or crossed a boundary you thought was clear. And when you tried to name it, when the heat rose in your chest and your voice sharpened, you were met with a serene smile and four words that landed like a slap: "Just let it go." Maybe it came from a yoga teacher. Maybe a therapist steeped in New Age platitudes. Maybe a friend who'd just finished a weekend workshop on manifestation. The message was the same: your anger is the problem. Your inability to release it is what's keeping you small, stuck, unenlightened. In my own practice, I've watched this pattern repeat with devastating consistency. People arrive carrying years of unexpressed rage, their bodies tight with it, their sleep fractured, their relationships strained. And underneath it all, a deep shame about the anger itself. They've been taught that spiritual maturity means rising above difficult emotions, that holding space for anger is somehow less evolved than choosing peace. This is spiritual bypassing, and it's one of the most insidious forms of emotional avoidance our culture has normalized. ## The Mechanics of Bypassing: How "Let It Go" Becomes a Weapon Spiritual bypassing, a term coined by psychologist John Welwood in the 1980s, describes the use of spiritual beliefs and practices to sidestep unresolved psychological issues. When we're told to "just let it go" before we've actually processed what happened, we're being asked to perform a kind of emotional amputation. The body doesn't work that way. Anger isn't a bad thought you can affirmation away. It's a physiological response to perceived threat or injustice, complete with elevated heart rate, tensed muscles, and a flood of stress hormones. When you're told to release it prematurely, you're essentially being asked to override your nervous system's assessment of danger without addressing the conditions that triggered it. Beverly Engel, who has written extensively on the relationship between anger and healing from abuse, points out that victims of mistreatment often need their anger as a crucial step toward reclaiming their sense of self. The anger says: what happened to you was wrong. You didn't deserve it. Your boundaries matter. When we bypass this step in the name of forgiveness or peace, we're asking people to abandon themselves all over again. The pressure to "let it go" often comes from people who are uncomfortable with your anger, not from any genuine concern for your wellbeing. Your anger might be asking them to acknowledge their complicity, to change their behavior, or simply to sit with discomfort. "Let it go" becomes a way to protect themselves from the demands your anger might make. ## What Anger Actually Carries: The Information You're Being Asked to Ignore Karla McLaren, who has done new work on emotional literacy, describes anger as a boundary-setting emotion. It arises when something important to you has been threatened or violated. The anger itself contains specific information: this matters to me. This line was crossed. This needs to change. When you're told to release anger before you've decoded its message, you lose access to that information. You might "let go" of your anger at a partner who repeatedly dismisses your needs, but without processing what that anger was telling you about the relationship, you'll simply recreate the same dynamic. The anger will return because the underlying issue remains unaddressed. Over the years I've seen this play out in predictable ways. Someone practices forgiveness meditation for months, genuinely believes they've released their anger at a parent or ex-partner, and then finds themselves inexplicably triggered by similar situations. They interpret this as their own failure, proof that they're not spiritual enough, not evolved enough. The real issue is that they were asked to skip steps. Your body knows what happened. Your nervous system remembers. The anger is there as a guardian, holding the memory of violation until you're ready to truly address it. Trying to transcend it prematurely just drives it deeper into your tissues, where it manifests as chronic tension, digestive issues, insomnia, or that constant low-grade anxiety you can't quite name. This is where understanding your baseline responses can help. If you've never mapped your own anger patterns, [the assessments hub](/assessments) offers several frameworks for understanding how your particular nervous system processes threat and boundary violations. ## The Body Keeps the Score: Where Bypassed Anger Lives Your jaw is the first place. It tightens imperceptibly, day after day, until you wake up with TMJ so severe you can barely open your mouth to eat. Your shoulders creep up toward your ears. Your digestion becomes erratic. You develop tension headaches that no amount of magnesium seems to touch, though [Doctor's Best Magnesium Glycinate Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DCGJ8HN4?tag=spankyspinola-20) can at least take the edge off the muscle spasms. These aren't random symptoms. They're your body's attempt to contain an emotion you've been told isn't acceptable. The anger has to go somewhere. If it can't move through voice, through action, through the natural arc of expression and resolution, it goes into tissue. I remember working with someone who'd spent years in a meditation practice focused on equanimity. She came in complaining of chronic neck pain and a feeling of constant pressure in her chest. When we started exploring her history, she described a pattern of boundary violations in her family of origin that would make anyone furious. But she'd been taught that anger was ego, that the goal was to see everyone as doing their best, to forgive and release. Her neck pain was literal. She couldn't turn her head to look at what had happened. Her chest pressure was the weight of unexpressed rage pressing against her sternum, looking for a way out. The pain itself was the anger, speaking in the only language it had left. The sensory experience of bypassed anger is distinct. It's a heaviness in the limbs, a sense of being both numb and hypervigilant at once. You might find yourself clenching your fists without realizing it, or grinding your teeth in your sleep. There's often a sensation of something stuck in the throat, words that were never allowed to be spoken now lodged like a stone. ## The Difference Between Suppression and Processing: What "Letting Go" Actually Requires Harriet Lerner, in her examination of anger in relationships, emphasizes that anger is neither good nor bad - it's simply information about our internal state and our evaluation of a situation. The goal isn't to eliminate anger but to use it intelligently, to let it inform our choices and actions. True release requires first making contact with the anger fully. This doesn't mean acting on it impulsively or using it to harm others. It means allowing yourself to feel the heat of it, to track where it lives in your body, to hear what it's trying to tell you. It means naming what happened without minimizing it. It means acknowledging that your anger makes sense given the circumstances. Only after you've truly heard the anger can you make an informed choice about what to do with it. Maybe it informs a difficult conversation. Maybe it helps you recognize a pattern you need to exit. Maybe it simply allows you to stop gaslighting yourself about what actually occurred. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to this distinction: processing isn't the same as performing. You can do all the "anger release" rituals in the world, but if you're doing them to get rid of an emotion you find unacceptable, you're still bypassing. The anger knows the difference. Real processing involves sitting with the discomfort. It involves writing the letter you'll never send, speaking the truth out loud to yourself or a trusted witness, letting your body shake or cry or move in whatever way it needs to. It involves acknowledging that yes, you're angry, and no, that doesn't make you unspiritual or unhealed. Research on emotional regulation, including work highlighted on the NIMH page on emotional regulation, consistently shows that attempts to suppress emotions lead to greater physiological arousal and poorer mental health outcomes than allowing emotions to be experienced and processed. ## A Five-Minute Practice: Making Contact With What You've Been Told to Release Find a private space where you won't be interrupted. Set a timer for five minutes. Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Close your eyes if that feels safe. Take three slow breaths, feeling your hands rise and fall. Now bring to mind a situation where you were told to "let it go" or "move on" from something that still bothers you. Don't try to change how you feel about it. Just notice what happens in your body when you think about it. Where does the sensation live? Your jaw? Your shoulders? Your gut? Name the location silently: "There's tension in my jaw. There's heat in my chest." Now speak directly to the anger, silently or aloud: "I'm listening. What do you need me to know?" Don't force an answer. Just create space for whatever arises. Sometimes it's a word. Sometimes it's an image. Sometimes it's just a sense of being seen. Stay with this for the remaining time. When the timer sounds, thank the anger for whatever it showed you. Write down one sentence about what you noticed. You don't have to do anything with this information yet. You're simply practicing the radical act of listening to something you've been told to silence. ## The Shadow Side of Positivity Culture: When Peace Becomes Violence The insistence on positive vibes only, on forgiveness as the highest virtue, on letting go as the marker of spiritual advancement - these ideas don't exist in a vacuum. They serve a function. They keep us compliant. They prevent us from asking difficult questions about power, about who benefits when we silence our anger, about what might change if we actually honored what our rage was pointing toward. When marginalized people are told to let go of anger about systemic oppression, when abuse survivors are pressured to forgive their abusers for the sake of their own healing, when workers are expected to smile through exploitation - this isn't spiritual guidance. It's social control dressed in the language of wellness. The people most often told to let go of their anger are the people whose anger is most threatening to existing power structures. Women who are angry about misogyny. People of color who are angry about racism. Queer people who are angry about discrimination. The message is clear: your anger makes others uncomfortable, so you need to manage it, transcend it, let it go. But what if your anger is the most sane response to an insane situation? What if letting it go would mean abandoning your own integrity? True spiritual practice doesn't ask you to bypass your humanity. It asks you to meet it fully, to bring consciousness to all of it - including the parts that are furious, that refuse to be gaslit, that insist something needs to change. The peace that comes from genuine processing is entirely different from the false calm of suppression. One is grounded and embodied. The other is brittle and effortful. ## Supporting Your Nervous System Through the Process: Practical Tools As you begin to make contact with anger you've been bypassing, your nervous system may need support. This isn't about dulling the emotion - it's about giving your body the resources it needs to process intense activation without becoming overwhelmed. GABA, the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter, can help modulate the intensity of your stress response. [Pure Encapsulations GABA (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B003PQVU9Q?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a clean source without unnecessary additives. B vitamins, particularly B6, support neurotransmitter synthesis and can help stabilize mood during periods of emotional processing - [NOW Vitamin B-6 (Pyridoxine HCl) 100 mg (250 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005P0XRK0?tag=spankyspinola-20) provides a straightforward option. But supplements are support, not solution. They can take the edge off so you can do the actual work. They can't do the work for you. Movement practices that allow for discharge are crucial. This might be shaking, dancing, hitting a pillow, running until you're spent. Your body needs permission to express what's been locked down. If you're not sure where to start, [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) includes several nervines that can help create a sense of safety as you begin to release stored tension. Sleep becomes even more important during active processing. Your brain consolidates emotional experiences during REM sleep. If you're not sleeping well, you're not processing effectively. Prioritize rest the way you'd prioritize any other medicine. ## Three Questions Readers Actually Ask ### Isn't holding onto anger just hurting myself? The phrase "holding onto anger is like drinking poison" assumes that feeling anger means being consumed by it indefinitely. But there's a difference between chronically ruminating on resentment and actively processing legitimate anger about something that harmed you. The former keeps you stuck. The latter moves you through. Your anger isn't hurting you - the pressure to deny it is. ### How do I know when I've actually processed anger versus just suppressing it? Processed anger resolves. You can think about the situation without your nervous system activating. You have clarity about what happened and what you need going forward. Suppressed anger lurks. It resurfaces at unexpected moments. Your body stays tense. You find yourself triggered by situations that remind you of the original violation. The difference is in your soma, not your story about it. ### Can't I just choose to forgive and move forward? You can choose anything. The question is whether that choice is coming from genuine resolution or from the belief that your anger is unacceptable. Real forgiveness, when it comes, is a gift you give yourself after you've fully acknowledged what happened. Premature forgiveness is a performance that costs you your own truth. If you're asking whether you *can* forgive, you're probably not ready to. And that's okay. ## The Long View: What Becomes Possible When You Stop Bypassing There's a version of you on the other side of this work. She's not transcendent in the way you were taught to aspire to. She's not above human emotion, floating in some permanent state of equanimity. She's something better: she's integrated. She feels her anger when it arises, tracks it to its source, uses it to inform her choices. She doesn't perform peace for others' comfort. She doesn't apologize for taking up space with her legitimate grievances. She's learned that anger, allowed to move through its natural cycle, actually resolves faster than anger that's been suppressed in the name of spiritual advancement. This doesn't mean she's constantly furious. It means she's honest. The anger comes, delivers its message, and moves through. She doesn't have to carry it as chronic tension because she's not forcing it underground. She's not exhausted from the effort of maintaining a false calm. She's not confused about why her body hurts when her mind insists everything is fine. The work of unlearning spiritual bypassing isn't comfortable. It asks you to feel what you've been avoiding, to question the teachings you've taken as truth, to risk being seen as unhealed or unspiritual by people who are still invested in bypassing their own pain. It asks you to trust your anger more than you trust the voices telling you to let it go. But on the other side of that discomfort is something you may have forgotten was possible: a life where you don't have to choose between your spiritual practice and your emotional truth. Where both can coexist. Where your anger can be sacred too. For more on the integration of shadow and light, [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) explores these themes through a different lens, and [more articles in this journal](/articles) continue these conversations across different contexts and practices. The invitation is simply this: what if your anger has been waiting not to be released, but to be heard? ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on the spiritual bypassing of 'just let it go', we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
